coredata.dc:title: Harnessing graphene quantum dots for the selective and sensitive detection of dye contaminants

Li, Weitao
Zhang, Luoman

Wang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: The increasingly serious environmental crisis of textile wastewater pollution has aroused widespread concern, mainly because it contains numerous anionic and cationic dyes, which poses a serious public health risk. Excessive exposure to anionic and cationic dyes can have detrimental health effects. So effective methods of detecting and removing these dyes are essential to mitigate these issues. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently garnered attention for their potential in dye detection due to their superior properties. Here, we use a fluorescent-based method for the sensitive and selective detection of cationic dyes. Cyan GQDs (c-GQDs) with a quantum yield of 29.86 %, high dispersion size of 4.13 nm and average thickness of 2.12 nm were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using 1-amino-2-naphthol 4-sulfonic acid as a precursor and acrylic acid as a regulator. The synthesized c-GQDs exhibit distinct cyan fluorescence at 465 nm upon excitation at 380 nm. A good relationship between fluorescence intensity and methylene blue (MLB) dye concentration was established by a linear regression analysis, and the detection limit of 0.025 μM was determined. The fluorescence quenching mechanism after the interaction between c-GQDs and MLB is consistent with the static quenching mechanism. Notably, c-GQDs showed high selectivity for MLB, highlighting its potential as effective fluorescent probes for detecting cationic dyes in aqueous environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Dye detection
Methylene blue
Wastewater

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62305400), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420423), Key Scientific Research Projects of Higher Education Institutions in Henan Province (25B540001), Young backbone teachers of Zhongyuan University of Technology (2024XQG04), the Discipline Young Master's Tutor Cultivation project of Zhongyuan University of Technology (SD202432), the Graduate Education Quality Improvement Project of Zhongyuan University of Technology (JG202323), Henan Provincial Science and Technology R&D Program Joint Fund (232103810035), Zhengzhou Key Laboratory Project (zzsffe202302), and Changsha Natural Science Foundation (kq2208392).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: b-GQDs (a) and c-GQDs (b) height distribution maps and AFM images (illustrated by b-GQDs and c-GQDs thickness distribution histograms and line plots). TEM images showing the relative sizes of the b-GQDs (c) and c-GQDs (d) (illustrated by the b-GQDs and c-GQDs size distribution histogram). TEM images with high resolution of b-GQDs (e) and c-GQDs (f) (illustration by Fast Fourier transform diagram).

XRD spectra for b-GQDs and c-GQDs (a). FT-IR spectra for b-GQDs and c-GQDs (b). Raman spectra for b-GQDs and c-GQDs (c). (d) The complete X-ray photoelectron spectrum of b-GQDs and c-GQDs. (e) the C1s spectrum acquired via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of b-GQDs and c-GQDs. (f) the N1s spectrum of b-GQDs and c-GQDs.
UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescent excitation, and emission spectra for b-GQDs (a) and c-GQDs (b). The fluorescent spectra of b-GQDs (c) and c-GQDs (d) at various excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) b-GQDs and (b) c-GQDs in different dye solutions (0.1 mM). (c) Histograms representing the fluorescence intensity of b-GQDs and c-GQDs in various dye solutions. (d) Fluorescence spectra of c-GQDs in the presence of MLB (0.1 mM) and different metal ions (0.1 mM). Images of b-GQDs and c-GQDs in different dye solutions under natural light (e) and ultraviolet light (f) (DI represents the spectrum of the quantum dots without the analyte.).

(a) The luminescence intensity of c-GQDs at different MLB concentrations ranged from 0.2 to 2 μM for MLB. Linear Stern-Volmer plot for MLB (The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements) (b) c-GQDs and (c) b-GQDs. (d) Fluorescence lifetime of c-GQDs before and after MLB addition. Exponential fitting of fluorescence lifetime of c-GQDs without MLB (e) and with MLB (f).
The synthesis and utilization of b-GQDs and c-GQDs for the detection of dyes.

head.ce:title: Harnessing graphene quantum dots for the selective and sensitive detection of dye contaminants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasingly serious environmental crisis of textile wastewater pollution has aroused widespread concern, mainly because it contains numerous anionic and cationic dyes, which poses a serious public health risk. Excessive exposure to anionic and cationic dyes can have detrimental health effects. So effective methods of detecting and removing these dyes are essential to mitigate these issues. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently garnered attention for their potential in dye detection due to their superior properties. Here, we use a fluorescent-based method for the sensitive and selective detection of cationic dyes. Cyan GQDs (c-GQDs) with a quantum yield of 29.86 %, high dispersion size of 4.13 nm and average thickness of 2.12 nm were synthesized via the hydrothermal method using 1-amino-2-naphthol 4-sulfonic acid as a precursor and acrylic acid as a regulator. The synthesized c-GQDs exhibit distinct cyan fluorescence at 465 nm upon excitation at 380 nm. A good relationship between fluorescence intensity and methylene blue (MLB) dye concentration was established by a linear regression analysis, and the detection limit of 0.025 μM was determined. The fluorescence quenching mechanism after the interaction between c-GQDs and MLB is consistent with the static quenching mechanism. Notably, c-GQDs showed high selectivity for MLB, highlighting its potential as effective fluorescent probes for detecting cationic dyes in aqueous environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing environmental crisis of textile wastewater pollution have drawn significant attention largely due to the uncontrolled release of harmful dyes into aquatic ecosystems. Both anionic and cationic dyes exhibit, high stability and resistance to natural degradation processes, and thus persist in the environment for long periods. Presenting significant threats to environmental health and human safety [1–3]. The persistent nature of these dyes, combined with their high chemical stability, makes their removal from wastewater a critical environmental challenge. Conventional water treatment methods, while somewhat effective, are typically costly, inefficient, for large-scale applications and lack the specificity required for selective dye removal [4–6]. Furthermore, many of these conventional approaches require high energy input or produce hazardous byproducts, which can lead to secondary environmental pollution. The need for innovative, cost-effective, and highly selective detection methods has, therefore, become increasingly urgent [7,8].

Among the various harmful dyes, MLB is particularly concerning due to its widespread use in the textile, pharmaceutical, and chemical industries. MLB is a cationic dye known for its high water solubility and its adverse effects on aquatic ecosystems and human health. Prolonged exposure to MLB can lead to gastrointestinal irritation, respiratory issues, and has been linked to more severe conditions such as cancer [9,10]. The removal of MLB from wastewater is, therefore, a pressing issue, not only for environmental safety but also for public health. Efficient removal of such dyes is crucial to mitigate these health risks [11,12]. And existing techniques for dye removal range from adsorption and chemical precipitation to advanced methods like photocatalysis [13–15]. However, these approaches often require a large number of samples and are expensive to test, involve complex operations, high maintenance costs, and often introduce secondary environmental contaminants, limiting their long-term applicability [16,17]. The fluorescence detection method adopted by us has the advantages of low cost, few samples required, simple, rapid and high sensitivity [18,19].

GQDs have recently garnered attention as a promising candidate for the environmental monitoring applications due to their nanoscale size, excellent chemical stability, water solubility, tunable photoluminescence properties, and biocompatibility [20–22]. One of the key advantages of GQDs is their ability to undergo surface functionalization, which allows for the introduction of various chemical groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, that can interact with specific pollutants, enhancing their selectivity and sensitivity for target dyes like MLB [23–25]. In this study, we explore the use of c-GQDs as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor for the detection of MLB in aqueous solutions. The c-GQDs were synthesized via a facile, bottom-up hydrothermal process, which allowed for the precise control of their size and optical properties. The resulting c-GQDs exhibited strong cyan fluorescence under UV light, making them ideal for fluorescence-based sensing applications. Through a series of spectroscopic and analytical techniques, we demonstrate that the c-GQDs exhibit remarkable selectivity toward MLB, even in the presence of other potentially interfering species. Furthermore, we investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, which contributes to the high sensitivity of the system. Our finding offers a sensitive, cost-effective platform for the detection of cationic dyes in aqueous environments.

All chemicals were procured and used as received, without any further purification. 1-Amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid, acrylic acid (AA), Azure B, malachite green (MG), rhodamine B (RB), rhodamine 6G (R6G), cationic brilliant red 4G (ABR4G), methyl orange (MO), methyl blue (MB), and methylene blue (MLB) were supplied by Shanghai Sinopod Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Additionally, ZnCl₂, CoCl₂, NaCl, KNO₃, LiCl, Cd(NO₃)₂, MnCl₂, NiCl₂, Al(NO₃)₃, and MgCl₂ were obtained from Sinopharm Group.

An array of advanced instruments was employed for the characterization and analysis of the synthesized GQDs. The fluorescent properties of GQDs were studied under high-intensity UV light (FC-100/FA, Spectroline, Melville, NY, USA). Raman spectral analysis was conducted using a confocal micro-Raman spectrometer (X-plora PLUS, Shanghai, China), and the height profiles of the GQDs were measured with an atomic force microscope (AFM) (MFP-3D Infinity, Oxford, UK). The infrared spectrum of the GQDs was recorded using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) (ALPHA II, Bruker, Germany), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a multi-purpose X-ray diffractometer (D2 PHASER, Bruker, Germany).

Fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan), while high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a TEM (JEOL JEM-F200, Japan Electronics Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The morphologies of GQDs and GQDs/MLB were obtained by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma 360, Germany). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a spectrophotometer (LAMBDA750, PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted using an X-ray diffractometer (K-Alpha, Europe, UK) to obtain atomic composition data. Fluorescence lifetime maps were obtained using a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS980, Edinburgh, UK).
Fluorescently tunable GQDs were synthesized using a solvothermal method, with 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid as the precursor and an acid as the regulator. The procedure is outlined as follows:

A solution was prepared by dissolving 0.59 g of 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid in a mixture of 5 mL of acrylic acid and 50 mL of deionized water. The solution was then subjected to ultrasonic treatment to ensure uniform dispersion. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined stainless steel autoclave and subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resultant solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble impurities, yielding a c-GQDs solution. For further analysis, the solution was dialyzed and dried at 70 °C to obtain solid c-GQDs powder. The c-GQDs solution was used without any additional purification.

For the synthesis of blue fluorescent GQDs, 0.59 g of 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. The remaining steps were identical to those used for the preparation of c-GQDs.

A 0.1 mM solutions of various metal ions (Mn2+, Cd2+, Ni+, K+, Al3+, Na+, Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Li+) and different dyes (MO, MB, MLB, MG, RB, R6G, Azure B, ABR4G) was prepared. For the fluorescence measurements, 500 μL of each ion or dye solution was mixed with 500 μL of b-GQDs or c-GQDs solution. These mixtures were then allowed to stand at room temperature for 12 h before conducting the fluorescence analysis. (We compare the experimental results of 0 h and 12 h, and find that the detection effect of 12 h is more in line with the actual situation, as shown in Fig. S1.)

For MLB detection, solutions with varying molar concentrations of MLB (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.5, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM) were prepared and mixed with the c-GQDs solution. Fluorescence intensity measurements were taken after a 12 h incubation period. Each fluorescence measurement was conducted in triplicate, and the results were consistent across the trials.

In this study, b-GQDs and c-GQDs were synthesized using 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid as a precursor and acrylic acid as a regulator in a bottom-up hydrothermal process, as shown in Scheme 1 . The morphological properties of both b-GQDs and c-GQDs were analyzed using TEM and AFM, as presented in Fig. 1 . The AFM analysis revealed that the b-GQDs and c-GQDs primarily composed of multilayer graphene structures, with thicknesses of approximately 2.85 nm and 2.12 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1a,b. The thinner structure of the c-GQDs can be attributed to the controlled surface functionalization achieved through the synthesis process, which reduces the overall layer stacking, resulting in improved surface reactivity and dispersibility [26,27].

TEM images (Fig. 1c,d) further revealed the nanodot morphology of both b-GQDs and c-GQDs, showing well-dispersed particle with relatively uniform size distributions. The average diameters of 3.54 nm for b-GQDs and 4.13 nm for c-GQDs were measured. The larger particle size of c-GQDs may be a result of surface modification through acrylic acid, leading to increased aggregation of graphene layers and thus larger nanodots. High-resolution TEM images (Fig. 1e,f) provided additional insights into the crystalline nature of both GQDs types, displaying well-defined lattice fringes that indicate a high degree of crystallinity. The observed lattice spacings are 0.22 nm for b-GQDs and 0.24 nm for c-GQDs. The slight increase in lattice spacing for c-GQDs can be attributed to the presence of surface functional groups, which may induce lattice distortion [28,29].

Fig. 2a illustrates that the [002] crystal plane spacings for b-GQDs and c-GQDs, measured at 3.40 Å and 3.42 Å, respectively. The larger interplanar spacing observed in c-GQDs can be attributed to surface modifications, particularly the introduction of functional groups that induce lattice expansion. The FT-IR spectra of b-GQDs and c-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2b. The spectra confirm the presence of amino and hydroxyl functional groups, which contribute to the strong hydrophilicity and enhanced stability of c-GQDs compared to b-GQDs. The absorption peaks at 3500–3300 cm−1 correspond to the O-H/N-H stretching vibrations [30,31], while the peaks at 1050 cm−1 and 659 cm−1 are attributed to the SO and SC stretching vibrations, respectively [32]. The peak near 1590 cm−1 represents the -C=C stretching vibration, characteristic of the graphene structure [33]. The CO stretching vibrations (from -COOH groups) are detected at 1283 cm−1 [34], while the peak at 1096 cm−1 indicates the sulfur chemical state, associated with the SO stretching vibration [35]. Further, peaks near 1700 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 represent the CO and CN stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of c-GQDs [36–38]. Raman spectra (Fig. 2c) further demonstrate the structural differences between b-GQDs and c-GQDs. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) is 0.842 for b-GQDs and slightly higher at 0.922 for c-GQDs, suggesting a lower degree of graphitization in c-GQDs [39,40]. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2d and Table S1) reveals that the b-GQDs are primarily composed of 68.62 % C (285 eV), 29.63 % O (532 eV), 0.92 % N (400 eV), and 1.09 % S (169 eV). Comparatively, c-GQDs display a reduction in nitrogen content alongside a slight increase in sulfur concentration, which can be ascribed to the decreased presence of electron-donating amino groups and the enrichment of electron-withdrawing sulfonic acid functionalities [41,42]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) is deconvoluted into four distinct peaks corresponding to C=C/C–C (285 eV), C–N/C–O (285.6 eV), CO (288 eV), and CS (289 eV) [43–46], while the O1s spectrum (Fig. S2a, c) reveals three peaks associated with OC (531 eV), OC (533 eV), and SO3 (536.5 eV) [43,44,47]. Notably, the proportion of CO in c-GQDs is higher relative to b-GQDs, signifying an increase in carboxyl groups, which are classified as weak electron-withdrawing moieties. Moreover, the S2p spectrum (Fig. S2b,d) substantiates the presence of SC bonds (169 eV), further corroborating the integration of sulfonic acid groups [48]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) presents a single peak corresponding to NH2 [49], in alignment with FT-IR analysis collectively. These spectral features indicate a reduction in electron-donating functionalities (−NH2) and an increase in electron-withdrawing groups (-SO3 and -COOH), thereby affirming the regulatory influence of AA in modulating the surface electronic structure of GQDs, ultimately inducing a red-shift in their photoluminescence properties [50,51].

Fig. 3a,b show the optical properties of GQDs prepared by hydrothermal method, illustrated with illustrations of GQDs under natural and ultraviolet light, alongside their respective fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelengths of 350 nm and 380 nm, and emission spectra recorded at 400 nm and 465 nm, respectively. Fig. 3c, d is the illustrate fluorescence spectra of b-GQDs and c-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity of b-GQDs and c-GQDs increased with the increase of excitation wavelength, reaching the strongest at 350 nm for b-GQDs and 380 nm for c-GQDs. Beyond these optimal wavelengths, the fluorescence intensity begins to decrease as the excitation wavelength increases. This behavior highlights distinct excitation-dependent fluorescence properties, with the optimal excitation wavelengths of b-GQDs and c-GQDs being 350 nm and 380 nm, corresponding to Fig. 3c, d. Due to their highly ordered graphitic structure, the fluorescence peak of the b-GQDs exhibits minor variations with increasing excitation wavelength. In contrast, the fluorescence emission of c-CQDs demonstrates a noticeable redshifted (Fig. S3). This redshift, a common phenomenon in fluorescent carbon materials, is possibly attributed to the presence of various energy traps on the surface of c-CQDs, which introduce a range of electronic states that can affect the emission energy. The quantum yield (QY) of both b-GQDs and c-GQDs was evaluated using quinine sulfate as the standard reference [52]. The QY of c-GQDs was measured at is 29.86 %, which is higher than the 15.49 % of b-GQDs.

The stability of the prepared GQDs systematically evaluated across pH, thermal, and temporal parameters. As shown in Fig. S4, pH stability tests revealed distinct behaviors for b-GQDs and c-GQDs, with initial pH values of 4 and 3, respectively. In Fig. S4a, the fluorescence intensity of b-GQDs exhibited a slight decline in acid solutions (pH 1–3). However, a more pronounced fluorescence quenching was observed in alkaline conditions (pH 5 and 7), indicating the possible formation of surface defects that reduce their photoluminescence efficiency. In contrast, Fig. S4b illustrates that c-GQDs maintain better fluorescence stability across a broader pH range. While a gradual decrease in fluorescence was noted in highly alkaline solutions (pH 13–14), c-GQDs displayed a more linear quenching response, demonstrating moderate alkaline resistance. Their response to acidic environments also indicated a gradual fluorescence reduction, with better acid tolerance than b-GQDs. The improved performance of c-GQDs compared to b-GQDs may be due to the incorporation of acrylic acid, which enhances their acid and alkali resistance.

Thermal stability was assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of GQDs before and after drying and redispersing in deionized water (Fig. S5). The fluorescence intensity of both c-GQDs and b-GQDs was generally steady before and after, indicating their good thermal stability. Additionally, temporal stability was examined over time, and as shown in Fig. S6, both GQDs types exhibited minimal degradation, with c-GQDs showing superior dispersion stability. These results collectively demonstrate the robust thermal and temporal stability of the GQDs, with c-GQDs exhibiting better overall stability than b-GQDs.

To evaluate the selectivity and sensitivity of GQDs-based fluorescent probes toward various dyes, a comprehensive study was conducted using different dye solutions. The fluorescence quenching behavior of b-GQDs and c-GQDs was systematically investigated, as depicted in Fig. 4a–f. While b-GQDs exhibited fluorescence quenching in the presence of MB, interference from other dyes was evident, resulting in an indistinct quenching effect relative to the control sample (Fig. 4a). In contrast, c-GQDs displayed pronounced and selective fluorescence quenching specifically toward methylene blue derivative MLB, with minimal interference from other dyes, indicating their strong selectivity for MLB detection (Fig. 4b). We compared the fluorescence intensity of b-GQDs and c-GQDs in different dye solutions (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the impact of metal ions commonly found in real-world water samples was assessed. The presence of metal ions showed no significant effect on c-GQDs performance (Fig. 4d), while certain ions interfered with the detection capability of b-GQDs (Fig. S7), suggesting that surface functionalization of c-GQDs enhanced their selectivity and minimized external interference.

Further analysis demonstrated a clear linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of c-GQDs and MLB concentration up to 2 μM (Fig. 5a,b), confirming the sensitivity of c-GQDs as a fluorescent sensor. Quenching behavior was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation, yielding a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9998), a quenching constant (KSV) of 0.15331 μM−1, and a detection limit (LOD) of 0.025 μM (The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of b-GQDs and MLB concentration is shown in Fig. 5c.). In addition, the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of b-GQDs and c-GQDs and the concentration of other dyes is shown in Fig. S8. The fluorescence lifetime of c-GQDs was determined to be 4.23 ns, and the corresponding quenching rate constant (Kq) was 36.11 × 1011 M−1 s−1. This unusually high Kq value suggests a static quenching mechanism, further corroborated by the observed decrease in fluorescence lifetime of c-GQDs from 5.74 ns to 4.23 ns upon MLB addition, as shown in the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves (Fig. 5d–f). We also conducted AFM and SEM tests, as shown in Figs. 1b and S9, which can well see the changes before and after adding MLB, which is well dispersed around c-GQDs. Moreover, c-GQDs retained their high quenching efficiency, stability, and reusability, making them promising candidates for practical applications in the selective detection of MLB. Comparisons with previously reported methods (Table S2) revealed that c-GQDs possess superior sensitivity and selectivity, establishing them as effective fluorescence probes for MLB in complex aqueous environments.

In order to verify the feasibility of c-GQDs fluorescent probe in practical application, the standard addition method was adopted. Different concentrations of MLB were added to unpurified tap water and the same concentration of c-GQDs fluorescent probe was employed for detection. The (F0 F) /F0 values were obtained and plugged into the linear fitting curve, and the specific concentration of the calculated MLB is compared with the concentration of the added MLB. The results are shown in Table S3. The analysis results show that the actual detection results are essentially consistent with the added MLB. The experimental results show that c-GQDs can be used for the detection of trace MLB in real water samples.

In conclusion, c-GQDs were adeptly synthesized via a bottom-up hydrothermal method, employing 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid as a precursor and acrylic acid as a modulator. This study elucidates a streamlined methodology for tailoring the photoluminescent properties of GQDs by modulating the electron-withdrawing and electron-donating groups on their surface through an acid reagent. The incorporation of a suitable acid regulator during synthesis effectively governs the surface electron-absorbing functionalities, thereby significantly influencing the fluorescence characteristics of GQDs. The resultant c-GQDs demonstrate excellent fluorescence stability, pronounced excitation wavelength dependence, and a remarkable quantum yield of up to 29.86 %. Concurrent selective dye recognition tests revealed the exceptional sensitivity and selectivity of c-GQDs toward MLB, with fluorescence quenching observed over a concentration range of 0.2–2 μM and a notably low detection limit of 0.025 μM. These findings underscore the potential of c-GQDs as highly efficient fluorescent probes for dye detection. Moreover, GQDs are anticipated to broaden their applications in environmental monitoring, particularly in the detection and remediation of hazardous dyes.

Yuping Xiong: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Weitao Li: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Luoman Zhang: Investigation. Boyu Li: Investigation. Shuangyan Li: Investigation. Qingguo Feng: Investigation. Jialin Liu: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Huan Wang: Investigation. Dandan Chen: Investigation. Yanfei Chen: Investigation. Diedie Wei: Investigation. Huazhang Guo: Investigation. Jiye Zhang: Investigation. Liang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62305400), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (232300420423), Key Scientific Research Projects of Higher Education Institutions in Henan Province (25B540001), Young backbone teachers of Zhongyuan University of Technology (2024XQG04), the Discipline Young Master's Tutor Cultivation project of Zhongyuan University of Technology (SD202432), the Graduate Education Quality Improvement Project of Zhongyuan University of Technology (JG202323), Henan Provincial Science and Technology R&D Program Joint Fund (232103810035), Zhengzhou Key Laboratory Project (zzsffe202302), and Changsha Natural Science Foundation (kq2208392).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111914.
